subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 15! In this unit you will become a detective of special numbers. Numbers are not all the same – some are odd, some are even, some are square, some hide below zero, and some divide neatly into others while some refuse to. Learning to recognise these properties gives you a powerful new way of seeing every number you meet. When Amina sorts 24 mangoes into equal boxes at the Hargeisa market, when Yusuf checks whether a temperature of −5°C is colder than −2°C, or when Halima works out that 81 camels can be arranged in a perfect 9 by 9 square, they are all using the ideas in this unit.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why do special numbers matter so much? They are everywhere in real life. Traders use even and odd numbers and divisibility to share goods fairly. Farmers use factors to arrange animals or trees in equal rows. Builders use square numbers when they tile a floor. Negative numbers describe temperatures below freezing and depths below sea level. When you become confident with special numbers, you gain a sharper sense of how numbers behave – and you are building the strong foundation you need for multiplication, division and fractions in the units to come.

As you work, keep a pencil and some paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the number lines, list the factors, sketch the square patterns, and say the answers out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas.
Order and compare numbers, including negative numbers.
Use the inequality symbols < and > correctly to compare numbers.
Identify and work with odd and even numbers.
Find the factors and list the multiples of a number.
Recognise and generate square numbers and describe their patterns.
Apply tests of divisibility for 2, 5, 10, 25, 50 and 100.
Use Venn diagrams to sort numbers according to their properties.

concepts.title: Ordering and Comparing Numbers
concepts.explanation: Some numbers are smaller than zero. We call them negative numbers, and we write them with a minus sign in front, like −1, −2 and −3. They are just as real as the numbers you already know, and they appear all around you: Temperatures below freezing – a cold desert night in Hargeisa might drop to −2°C.
concepts.example: Depths below sea level – a fish swimming 5 metres down is at −5 metres.

concepts.title: Odd and Even Numbers
concepts.explanation: Every whole number is either odd or even. This is the simplest way of all to sort numbers, and it depends only on the last digit. Even numbers can be divided exactly by 2, with nothing left over. If you try to share an even number of dates between two friends, each gets a fair share and none is left. Even numbers always end in 0, 2, 4, 6 or 8. Examples: 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14 and so on.
concepts.example: Odd numbers cannot be divided exactly by 2 – there is always exactly one left over. Odd numbers always end in 1, 3, 5, 7 or 9. Examples: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15 and so on.

concepts.title: Factors and Multiples
concepts.explanation: These two words sound similar but mean opposite things, so learn them carefully. A factor divides *into* a number; a multiple is what you get when you multiply a number *up*. Factors are numbers that divide exactly into another number, leaving no remainder. Think of factors as the numbers of equal rows you could arrange a group into. The factors of 12 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6 and 12, because:
concepts.example: 12 ÷ 1 = 12, and 12 ÷ 12 = 1 (every number has 1 and itself as factors)

concepts.title: Square Numbers
concepts.explanation: A square number is what you get when you multiply a number by itself. We write ‘multiplied by itself’ using a small raised 2, called ‘squared’. So 3 squared, written 3², means 3 × 3 = 9. Here are the first ten square numbers: They are called square numbers because you can arrange exactly that many dots or tiles into a perfect square. Four dots make a 2 by 2 square; nine dots make a 3 by 3 square; sixteen dots make a 4 by 4 square. This is why a farmer who plants 81 trees can plant them in a perfect square of 9…
concepts.example: Why does adding the next odd number always make the next square? Picture growing a square of tiles. To turn a 3 by 3 square into a 4 by 4 square, you add a new row of 3 tiles along the bottom, a new column of 3 tiles up…

concepts.title: Tests of Divisibility
concepts.explanation: A number is divisible by another number if it can be divided by it with no remainder. For example, 15 is divisible by 3 because 15 ÷ 3 = 5 exactly, but 17 is not divisible by 4 because 17 ÷ 4 = 4 remainder 1. Rather than doing long division every time, you can use quick divisibility tests that look only at the last one or two digits. Here are the tests you need for this unit:
concepts.example: Divisible by 2: the last digit is 0, 2, 4, 6 or 8 (it is even). So 38, 124 and 500 all pass.

concepts.title: Sorting Numbers with Venn Diagrams
concepts.explanation: A Venn diagram uses overlapping rings to sort things by their properties. Each ring stands for one property. A number goes inside a ring if it has that property, in the overlap if it has both properties, and outside both rings if it has neither. Suppose we sort numbers into ‘factors of 30’ and ‘factors of 40’. The factors of 30 are 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 10, 15, 30. The factors of 40 are 1, 2, 4, 5, 8, 10, 20, 40. A number like 6 is a factor of 30 only, so it goes in the left ring alone. A number like 8 is a factor of 40…
concepts.example: Venn diagrams are powerful because they let you see, at a glance, what two groups of numbers share and how they differ. You will use them to sort by many properties: odd and even, multiples of 3 and square numbers,…

methods.title: How to find all the factors of a number
methods.example: Put these in order from smallest to largest: 3, −5, 0, −2, 7.
steps: Start with 1 and the number itself – these are always factors.
Test 2, 3, 4 and so on, writing each factor pair you find.
Stop when the pairs meet in the middle.
List all the factors in order from smallest to largest.

methods.title: How to compare or order numbers with negatives
methods.example: Write the correct symbol between −4 and −7.
steps: Picture the number line, with negatives on the left of zero.
The number further to the left is smaller.
Read from left to right for smallest-to-largest order.
Write < or > with the open mouth facing the bigger number.

methods.title: How to test divisibility
methods.example: Write the correct symbol between −4 and −7.
steps: For 2, 5 and 10, look only at the last digit.
For 25, 50 and 100, look at the last two digits.
Remember a number divisible by 10 is also divisible by 2 and 5.

methods.title: Find all the factors
steps: Solution: Work in pairs.
The next whole number, 5, does not divide 24, and 6 has already appeared, so we stop.
The factors of 24 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12 and 24 – eight factors in all.

methods.title: List multiples
methods.example: Write the first eight multiples of 6.
steps: Each one is 6 more than the last.
Check the list multiples result carefully.

methods.title: Square numbers
methods.example: Which square numbers lie between 50 and 100?
steps: Solution: List the squares: 49 (7²), 64 (8²), 81 (9²), 100 (10²).
The ones strictly between 50 and 100 are 64 and 81.
(49 is below 50, and 100 is the boundary.)

workedExamples.title: Order numbers including negatives
workedExamples.prompt: Put these in order from smallest to largest: 3, −5, 0, −2, 7.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Imagine the number line. The negatives sit left of zero, with −5 furthest left, then −2. Then comes 0, then the positives 3 and 7. Reading left to right gives −5, −2, 0, 3, 7.

workedExamples.title: Compare with < or >
workedExamples.prompt: Write the correct symbol between −4 and −7.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: On the number line, −4 is to the right of −7 (it is closer to zero), so −4 is the larger number. We write −4 > −7. The crocodile mouth opens towards −4. Worked Example 3: Odd or even? Is 1,998 odd or even? Is 505 odd or even? Solution: Look only at the last digit. 1,998 ends in 8, which is even, so 1,998 is even. 505 ends in 5, which is odd, so 505 is odd.

workedExamples.title: Odd or even?
workedExamples.prompt: Write the correct symbol between −4 and −7.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: On the number line, −4 is to the right of −7 (it is closer to zero), so −4 is the larger number. We write −4 > −7. The crocodile mouth opens towards −4. Worked Example 3: Odd or even? Is 1,998 odd or even? Is 505 odd or even? Solution: Look only at the last digit. 1,998 ends in 8, which is even, so 1,998 is even. 505 ends in 5, which is odd, so 505 is odd.

workedExamples.title: Find all the factors
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Work in pairs. 1 × 24, 2 × 12, 3 × 8, 4 × 6. The next whole number, 5, does not divide 24, and 6 has already appeared, so we stop. The factors of 24 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12 and 24 – eight factors in all.

workedExamples.title: List multiples
workedExamples.prompt: Write the first eight multiples of 6.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiply 6 by 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8: 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, 36, 42, 48. Each one is 6 more than the last.

workedExamples.title: Square numbers
workedExamples.prompt: Which square numbers lie between 50 and 100?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: List the squares: 49 (7²), 64 (8²), 81 (9²), 100 (10²). The ones strictly between 50 and 100 are 64 and 81. (49 is below 50, and 100 is the boundary.)

workedExamples.title: Tests of divisibility
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The last digit is 0, so it passes 2, 5 and 10. The last two digits are 00, which is in every list, so it also passes 25, 50 and 100. So 2,500 is divisible by all six.

workedExamples.title: Combine properties in a puzzle
workedExamples.prompt: I am a two-digit number. I am divisible by 5. I am odd. What could I be?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divisible by 5 means I end in 0 or 5. But I must also be odd, and numbers ending in 0 are even, so I must end in 5. Any two-digit number ending in 5 works: 15, 25, 35, 45, 55, 65, 75, 85 or 95.

workedExamples.title: Fill in a missing number
workedExamples.prompt: Fill in a missing number: a) −8 < __ < −6 b) −2 < __ < 0.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: For each number, list every property that applies (odd, even, multiple of 5, multiple of 10, square number): 25, 30, 64, 100.
workedExamples.solution: 25: odd, multiple of 5, square; 30: even, multiple of 5, multiple of 10; 64: even, square; 100: even, multiple of 5, multiple of 10, square.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Copy and complete a Venn diagram for ‘multiples of 3’ and ‘square numbers’ using: 4, 8, 9, 16, 20, 25, 36.
workedExamples.solution: From 4, 8, 9, 16, 20, 25, 36 – square only: 4, 16, 25; both a multiple of 3 and square: 9, 36; multiple of 3 only: none; neither: 8, 20.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: I am a three-digit number. I am divisible by 2 and by 25. The sum of my digits is 10. What could I be?
workedExamples.solution: 1) Divisible by 2 and 25 means divisible by 50, so the number ends in 00 or 50. Checking the three-digit multiples of 50 for a digit sum of 10, the only answer is 550 (5 + 5 + 0 =

practice.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

activities.title: Factor Bug Hunt
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a handful of stones or beans.
steps: Choose a number such as 12, 18 or 24 and count out that many stones.
Arrange the stones into equal rows in every way you can – each arrangement shows a pair of factors.
Write the number in the middle of your page and draw its factors branching out like the legs of a bug.
Which number in your family has the most factors? Investigate 12, 24 and 36.
Keep your factor bugs – they show at a glance which numbers can be shared many ways.

activities.title: Build the Square Numbers
activities.materials: small square tiles, bottle caps, or stones.
steps: Make a 1 by 1 square (1 tile), then a 2 by 2 square (4 tiles), then 3 by 3, then 4 by 4.
Write down how many tiles each square uses: 1, 4, 9, 16 …
Work out the difference between each square and the next. What sequence do you get?
Predict how many tiles a 6 by 6 square needs, then build it to check.

activities.title: The Divisibility Sorting Game
activities.materials: number cards you write yourself (for example 35, 70, 125, 150, 250, 500).
steps: Draw three baskets on paper labelled ‘divisible by 5’, ‘divisible by 10’ and ‘divisible by 25’.
Turn over each card and decide which baskets it belongs in – some belong in more than one!
Use only the last one or two digits to decide, not long division.
Ask a family member to check your sorting and explain your reasoning to them.
This game trains you to use the quick divisibility tests with confidence.

activities.title: Number Line Temperatures (Family Task)
activities.materials: paper and a pencil to draw a long number line from −10 to 10.
steps: Mark zero in the middle, negatives to the left and positives to the right.
Ask family members to call out temperatures, and place each one on your line.
Order the temperatures from coldest to warmest by reading the line left to right.
Work out the difference between the coldest and the warmest temperature.

activities.title: Venn Diagram Number Sort (Investigation)
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and the numbers 1 to 30 written on small cards.
steps: Draw two large overlapping rings and label them ‘even numbers’ and ‘multiples of 3’.
Place each number card where it belongs, testing it against both rings.
Which numbers land in the overlap? What do 6, 12, 18, 24 and 30 have in common?
Try again with different labels, such as ‘square numbers’ and ‘odd numbers’.

activities.title: Special Number Treasure Hunt (Family Game)
activities.materials: your home, a street, or a market full of numbers.
steps: Hunt for numbers on doors, prices, pages and packets.
For each number you find, say one special thing about it: odd, even, square, a multiple of 5, and so on.
Score a point for every correct property you can name.
Play with the whole family and see who can spot a square number first.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand factors, squares or divisibility best? What was the trickiest part? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Number Line Rule
rules.text: Further LEFT means smaller. Further RIGHT means larger. So -8 < -3, and 2 > -4. The open mouth of < or > points at the bigger number.

rules.title: Odd and Even Rule
rules.text: Even numbers end in 0, 2, 4, 6 or 8. Odd numbers end in 1, 3, 5, 7 or 9. Zero is even. Just look at the last digit.

rules.title: Factors and Multiples Rule
rules.text: Factors divide into a number and are never bigger than it (find them in pairs). Multiples are the times-table of a number and are never smaller than it.

rules.title: Key rule 4
rules.text: Divisibility Rules By 2: last digit 0, 2, 4, 6, 8. By 5: last digit 0 or 5. By 10: last digit 0. By 25: last two digits 00, 25, 50, 75. By 50: 00 or 50. By 100: 00.

terms: Divides exactly into a number, no remainder
Result of multiplying by a whole number
Overlapping rings that sort by property
commonMistakes: Factors fit inside; multiples grow out
Zero shares into two equal groups: it is even
For 25, last two digits must be 00, 25, 50 or 75

realProblems.prompt: At 5:00 in the morning a thermometer in Nairobi reads −2°C. It rises 6 degrees by midday, then falls 9 degrees by night. What is the night-time temperature?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find two square numbers that add up to 65.
Find three consecutive even numbers that have a sum of 42.
How many numbers between 100 and 200 are divisible by 25? List them.
Use the digits 3, 5, 7 and 9 once each to make the largest possible even number, and the smallest possible odd number.
Explain the key idea in Sorting Numbers with Venn Diagrams.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A Venn diagram uses overlapping rings to sort things by their properties. Each ring stands for one property. A number goes inside a ring if it has that property, in the overlap if it has both properties, and outside both rings if it has neither. Suppose we sort numbers into ‘factors of 30’ and ‘factors of 40’. The factors of 30 are 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 10, 15, 30. The factors of 40 are 1, 2, 4, 5, 8, 10, 20, 40. A number like 6 is a factor of 30 only, so it goes in the left ring alone. A number like 8 is a factor of 40…

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why do special numbers matter so much? They are everywhere in real life. Traders use even and odd numbers and divisibility to share goods fairly. Farmers use factors to arrange animals or trees in equal rows. Builders use square numbers when they tile a floor. Negative numbers describe temperatures below freezing and depths below sea level. When you become confident with special numbers, you gain a sharper sense of how numbers behave – and you are building the strong foundation you need for multiplication, division and fractions in the units to come.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and some paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the number lines, list the factors, sketch the square patterns, and say the answers out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas.
I can order and compare numbers, including negative numbers.
I can use the inequality symbols < and > correctly to compare numbers.
I can identify and work with odd and even numbers.
I can find the factors and list the multiples of a number.
I can recognise and generate square numbers and describe their patterns.
